  • What are peptides, are they safe and is there evidence to back up the hype?

The medicines regulator is investigating whether UK clinics are breaking the law by making claims about the benefits of unregulated, experimental peptide therapies, the Guardian can reveal.

Interest in experimental peptides has boomed in recent years. The substances are delivered by injection and are touted by sellers, influencers and even some medics as aiding everything from anti-ageing to recovery from injury.

Storm Dave is expected to cause travel disruption this Easter weekend, with warnings for heavy snow and gale-force winds issued across northern parts of the UK, but a reprieve from the cold snap could be on the way, with temperatures forecast to reach the mid-20s next week.

The Met Office has issued a yellow severe weather warning in Scotland for heavy snow and blizzards causing some travel and power disruption. Up to 30 centimetres of snow could fall. An amber weather warning for wind has been issued for parts of northern England, Scotland and Wales on Saturday evening.

The video that started it all was innocuous enough: a woman in her 20s posted on TikTok about how she spends a perfect weekend in north London. On her list were the bakeries Jolene and Gail’s, and the De Beauvoir Deli.

The reaction, however, was anything but. Many locals commented that they had never heard of the businesses she mentioned. In Lebanon, the dead are usually given one last glimpse of their home town before they are laid to rest. Hoisted high above the heads of the living, their casket is slowly marched through the streets where they grew up.

It is the hands of their loved ones that guide them into their final resting place, already dug, and gently sprinkle dirt on their body.

items.title: Cubans study oil tanker diplomacy for signs of progress in secret talks with US
items.content_html:
Despite hostile rhetoric Trump let a Russian ship break his blockade – could it herald a Venezuela-style outcome?

When a sanctioned Russian oil tanker, the Anatoly Kolodkin, docked at Cuba’s Matanzas oil terminal on Tuesday, unloading 700,000 barrels of crude, it was not immediately clear why the ship had been allowed to pass through Donald Trump’s oil blockade.

In January, the US president had proclaimed on social media: “THERE WILL BE NO MORE OIL OR MONEY GOING TO CUBA – ZERO!” yet last week he told reporters, “If a country wants to send some oil into Cuba right now, I have no problem with it” – and waved the Russian ship through.

An Italian council has bought a villa where Benito Mussolini spent his summer holidays, partly to avoid the property falling into the hands of “fascist nostalgics”.

items.content_html:
Min Aung Hlaing seized control five years ago and plunged Myanmar into conflict and economic chaos

Min Aung Hlaing, the military general who plunged Myanmar into conflict and economic chaos when he took power in the 2021 coup has been appointed president, months after widely condemned sham elections.

Min Aung Hlaing, who is wanted by the prosecutor of the international criminal court for crimes against humanity against the Rohingya Muslim minority, was voted president by lawmakers on Friday. Myanmar’s parliament is dominated by the pro-military party, which won a landslide in one-sided elections earlier this year.

Tania Warner and her daughter Ayla Luca, originally from British Columbia, are both Canadian citizens. Warner moved to the US in 2021 when she married Edward Warner, a US citizen. “Very happy to have my family home … it’s been a whirlwind day,” said Edward Warner.

The Cook Islands was a dependent New Zealand colony from 1901-65 but has since operated as a self-governing nation in “free association” with New Zealand. Its roughly 17,000 citizens hold New Zealand citizenship. There are obligations between the two nations to regularly consult on matters of defence and security.

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox

Several news outlets have falsely reported that Somaliland’s government called for the extradition of Ilhan Omar, basing their stories on a post from an X account that does not represent the state despite its claims to the contrary.

Fox News, the New York Post, Sinclair Broadcast Group’s the National News Desk and the Independent ran stories on the US representative. The reports centred on a post by @RepOfSomaliland in reaction to claims by JD Vance that Omar had committed immigration fraud, which echoed prior allegations against the Somali-born Minnesota Democrat that she has vehemently denied.

items.content_html: Austria is set to follow other countries in curbing young people's access to social media. The three-party governing coalition plans to present a draft law by the end of June.

items.title: 1 Killed in Ukrainian Drone Strike on Belgorod Region
items.content_html: Belgorod region Governor Vyacheslav Gladkov said one drone struck a vehicle in the border village of Malomikhaylovka, killing the driver.

items.title: Russian Government Aims to Reopen Black Sea Beaches by June After Oil Spill Cleanup
items.content_html: Swimming has been banned around the resort city of Anapa in the Krasnodar region since thousands of metric tons of heavy fuel oil, known as mazut, washed ashore in December 2024.

source.name: The Moscow Times - Independent News From Russia

items.title: Germany: Mental health patients struggle to get help
items.content_html: Waiting lists for psychotherapy are full-to-bursting in Germany, with patients often waiting more than a year for treatment. Now a row has erupted over planned fee cuts for therapists in the public healthcare system.

items.title: Daylight saving time: A history of fact, folklore and fuss
items.content_html: From wartime strategy to Halloween lobbying lore, the legacy of daylight saving time is tangled in culture, commerce and contested claims.

items.title: Fighting in Iran: Is another 1970s‑style oil crisis looming?
items.content_html: Supply disruptions, high prices, nervous markets: The current war in the Middle East brings back memories of the oil crises of the 1970s. Back then, many economies plunged into stagflation. How bad is the current crisis?

items.title: Merz criticized for selective approach to international law
items.content_html: Dozens of legal scholars have called on the German government to condemn the US operations in Iran and Venezuela. They argue that if violations of international law go unpunished, the rule of law erodes.

items.title: Nepal’s PM-to-be uses rap to call for unity in first post-election message
items.content_html:
Balendra Shah, 35, is a symbol of change in country whose government was toppled last year in youth-led uprising

Nepal’s rapper turned politician Balendra Shah, who is about to be sworn in as prime minister, has issued his first post-election message in the form of a rap urging unity.

Hours before the release he swore an oath as a newly elected lawmaker, and he is due to become the Himalayan republic’s new prime minister on Friday.

items.title: European NATO defense spending rose by almost 20% in 2025
items.content_html: European NATO members and Canada increased their defense spending by an aggregate of 19.6% in 2025, the second consecutive year at that rate. German defense spending, famously sluggish to rise, reached 2.4% of GDP.

items.title: 'Looksmaxxing' — the manosphere beauty cult
items.content_html: The online trend with roots in misogyny encourages socially isolated young men to undertake painful practices to gain beauty and status — and its language is reaching the White House.

items.title: ​Germany: Building sector warns of spiralling costs
items.content_html: Germany has a desperate shortage of affordable housing. The government's modernization of the construction law seems to be failing to get more homes built at "turbo" speed.

items.title: Lufthansa and the role of big business in the Holocaust
items.content_html: German airline Lufthansa is one of many big-name brands that collaborated with the Nazis during the Holocaust. As the airline celebrates its 100th anniversary, why does its Nazi past continue to fly under the radar?

items.title: Smart sleepers could be the glow-up night trains need
items.content_html: Europe has a bit of an on-off relationship with night trains. A Berlin team's smart cabin concept could lead to greater commitment.

items.title: World Cup 2026: Cost, Trump and travel give fans pause
items.content_html: As teams battle it out for the final six spots, loyal fans are making plans for World Cup 2026. DW spoke to supporters across the world to hear about five-figure expenditure, visa uncertainty and safety concerns.

items.title: Germany's Left Party rocked by antisemitism allegations
items.content_html: In a resolution, a state‑level branch of the socialist Left Party has rejected the "Zionism that actually exists today." Are they denying Israel's right to exist? Is the resolution antisemitic?

items.title: Germany debates rape law to tackle AI and online abuse
items.content_html: Calls are growing for Germany to update its rape law after a celebrity case involving "deepfakes" caused an uproar. Campaigners are also pushing for a move to the "only yes means yes" principle of active consent.

items.title: Iran war boosts strategic significance of South Caucasus
items.content_html: With war raging in Iran, the South Caucasus has become one of the few stable corridors between Asia and Europe. Nowhere are the conflict's opportunities and risks more visible than in Azerbaijan.

items.title: Germany's green reputation hits a crossroads
items.content_html: Germany's new climate spend is big on wind power and e‑mobility. Some call it real momentum, others say it’s basically a green band‑aid.

items.title: Is VW eyeing Iron Dome parts production at German plant?
items.content_html: A report in the Financial Times says Volkswagen is considering producing defense components for Israel. A VW spokesperson told DW the company was exploring "viable options."

items.title: Is Iran cashing in millions from Strait of Hormuz blockade?
items.content_html: An Iranian lawmaker claims Tehran is quietly charging tankers up to $2 million for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz. The move threatens to turn the key waterway into a high-stakes toll booth.

items.title: From Goethe to Soraya: German-Iranian stories
items.content_html: It's hard to imagine now, but Iran and Germany were once surprisingly close. A master poet, a German "Lawrence of Arabia," and an empress all played a role in this shared history.

items.title: WNBA's labor deal is a structural shift for women's sports
items.content_html: America's top women's basketball league just agreed to a new CBA. It will have a significant impact on women's sport.

items.title: Taliban release US academic held in detention for more than a year
items.content_html:
Marco Rubio welcomes release of Dennis Coyle, who was detained in January last year for violating unspecified laws

Afghanistan’s Taliban authorities have released the American academic Dennis Coyle after holding him for over a year, with the foreign ministry saying the release came on the occasion of Eid al-Fitr, the Muslim holiday that marks the end of the Islamic holy month of Ramadan.

A statement from the ministry said the academic researcher had been released in Kabul on Tuesday, following an appeal from his family and after Afghanistan’s supreme court “considered his previous imprisonment sufficient”.

items.title: NASA plans moon base instead of orbital lunar station
items.content_html: New NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman says the US space agency will pivot from launching an orbiting lunar station to constructing a base on the moon's surface. It comes amid a renewed moon race with Russia and China.

items.title: Who wins what in the EU's new trade deal with Australia?
items.content_html: Squeezed by Trump's tariffs and China's industrial muscle, the EU and Australia bury old trade disputes to finalize a long-delayed free trade deal.

items.title: American apocalypse: The end 'feels personal and imminent'
items.content_html: Social psychologists in the US found that a third of people surveyed feel the world will end in their lifetimes. That's not only depressing; it also affects people's sense of agency when faced by war or climate change.

items.title: Germany's AfD: The new neoliberal workers' party?
items.content_html: Workers in Germany are increasingly voting for the far-right, populist Alternative for Germany party. That is despite the fact the party's platform is not even particularly worker-friendly.

items.title: Germany laments Trump's 'avoidable, unnecessary' Iran war

items.content_html: The European Union and Australia have agreed on a sweeping new trade deal, compromising to boost exports in the face of global uncertainty over trade.

items.title: Iran's energy sites still on Trump's target list
items.content_html: US President Trump has delayed strikes on Iran's power plants for five days, citing "productive" talks with Tehran. DW asks what infrastructure US forces could still bomb to force a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: How Africa gets shortchanged in trade with Europe
items.content_html: The establishment of free trade between much of both continents for half a century has created growth in many areas. However, the data analysis shows that Africa remains on unequal footing.

items.title: Alexandra Popp and Borussia Dortmund: A sign of the times
items.content_html: Germany's most famous female footballer has signed for a third-division team. The transfer will have huge impacts across the women's game in Germany.

items.content_html: German automakers are in a crisis due to stagnant sales, falling profits and constant strategy shifts. Can the industry recover?

items.title: Amazonia's Indigenous peoples dismantle Western cliches
items.content_html: Indigenous peoples and the Amazon rainforest have often been exoticized. A new exhibition challenges these long-held tropes.

items.content_html: Despite her age, Yayoi Kusama's art and its subject matter remain highly relevant. A retrospective of the Japanese artist's work is now on show at Museum Ludwig in Cologne.

items.content_html: Julian Nagelsmann's latest Germany squad includes a host of new names. The 2026 World Cup is less than three months away.

items.content_html: Cities are quietly becoming raw‑material hubs as urban miners add rubble to the carbon‑saving construction supply chain.

items.content_html: After Senegal were stripped of their title and Morocco were crowned winners, African football faces some big questions.

items.content_html: Rising tension in the Middle East is rattling high-end chipmakers. Among the latest geopolitical uncertainties for the industry is a shortage of helium that could slow global production.

items.title: The internet was supposed to be free. What went wrong?
items.content_html: Thirty years ago, countercultural thinkers declared the internet an independent space. Today, it's controlled by a handful of corporations. Were we deceived?

items.content_html: Gulf states like Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the UAE have invested, or promised to invest, trillions around the world, everywhere from America to Africa. The Iran war may see their money spent closer to home.

items.content_html: A growing number of researchers are turning to Pompeii's graffiti to better understand the lives, humor and concerns of ordinary people nearly 2,000 years ago. Spoiler alert: They liked gladiators.

items.content_html: Gold is typically seen as a safe haven, especially in times of war and other crises. Notwithstanding the ongoing war in Iran, the price is steady. What's driving the stability?

items.content_html: Donald Trump has mobilized around right-wing coded sports like MMA and wrestling, but his soccer World Cup push is a cultural clash.

items.title: Formula 1 in 2026: Just Formula E on steroids?

items.title: Ecosystem collapse could fuel next global security crisis

items.title: F1's Laura Müller continuing to blaze a trail for women
items.content_html: Laura Müller is Formula 1's first female race engineer, taking crucial decisions for Haas driver Esteban Ocon. On International Women's Day, Turn 6 at Melbourne's Albert Park race track is to be named after her.
